text: Metal–organic frameworks in heat transformation: adsorption and regeneration behaviour focused on water and methanol adsorbents Metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) are one of the new classes of materials that have been recently explored extensively for heat transformation processes like adsorption chillers and heat pumps. The potential of MOFs for applications of thermally driven adsorption chillers (TDCs) and adsorption heat pumps (AHPs) has triggered increasing interest during the last few years. The application of MOFs is supported by the fact that their high porosity and adsorption capacity can be tailored by choosing different ligands. Thus, it is possible to prepare MOFs that show high adsorption capacities for working fluids (e.g. water and/or methanol) compared to classical adsorbents like silica gels or zeolites. Additionally, the s-shaped isotherms of MOFs can facilitate a lower and higher temperature adsorption, and a low regeneration enthalpy. This focus article gives an overview on MOFs of MOFs with respect to their applications in heat transformation processes. It presents the water and methanol adsorption performance of MOFs in relation to classical adsorbents. Furthermore, the influences of hydrophilicity through the functionalisation of MOF linkers and their long-term hydrothermal stability for at least thousands of thermal cycles are discussed. The article also focuses on the material shaping of adsorbents and coatings of heat exchangers that improve the heat and mass transfer. The latter has to be increased to improve the power density of future adsorption-based thermal systems.
explanation: The passage is technical and coherent, which AI could generate. Yet it contains awkward phrasing ('MOFs of MOFs') that may result from editing or human error, making the AI authorship plausible but not certain.
